steps.title: Identify the lower bound of g(x)
steps.content:
We note that $2^{x}\gt 0$ for all real $x$.
Multiplying by 6 and adding 5 gives $6\cdot2^{x}+5\gt 5$.
Dividing by 17 yields $$ g(x) = \frac{1}{17}(6\cdot2^{x}+5) \gt \frac{5}{17}.$$
Thus $g(x)$ is always greater than $\frac{5}{17}$.

steps.title: Determine the greatest possible k
steps.content:
As $x\to -\infty$, $2^{x}\to 0^{+}$, so $$ g(x) \to \frac{1}{17}(6\cdot0+5) = \frac{5}{17}$$ from above.
Hence $g(x)$ can be made arbitrarily close to $\frac{5}{17}$ but never below it.
If $k \gt \frac{5}{17}$, choose $x$ sufficiently negative to make $g(x) \lt k$, violating the condition.
If $k = \frac{5}{17}$, then $g(x) \gt k$ for every $x$.
Therefore the greatest possible constant $k$ is $\frac{5}{17}$.
That matches Choice C — $\frac{5}{17}$.
